qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.title: Malformed ASN.1 length fields trigger CPU exhaustion during parsing
qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.advisory_match_reason: The advisory is a DSA private-key-recovery flaw caused by incomplete bound checks in `KJUR.crypto.Util.getRandomBigIntegerZeroToMax` and `getRandomBigIntegerMinToMax`, which bias nonces used by `KJUR.crypto.DSA.signWithMessageHash`. This finding instead targets ASN.1 length handling in `ASN1HEX.getVblen`/`getChildIdx` and X.509 parsing, so it is a different root cause and code path.

target_advisory_judgement.reason: No qualifying finding identifies the advisory-described DSA nonce-generation bug. The only qualifying finding is an ASN.1 parser availability issue in different files and functions than the advisory's incomplete-compareTo bug in DSA nonce generation.
evidence: The advisory summary and details describe private-key recovery through biased DSA nonces caused by incomplete `compareTo` checks in `getRandomBigIntegerZeroToMax` and `getRandomBigIntegerMinToMax` in `src/crypto-1.1.js`.

The bundled jsrsasign source used by the session shows `KJUR.crypto.DSA.signWithMessageHash` obtaining its nonce through `KJUR.crypto.Util.getRandomBigIntegerMinToMax(...)`, which is the advisory's code path.

The only qualifying finding report and PoC target `ASN1HEX.getVblen`, `ASN1HEX.getChildIdx`, and `X509.readCertHex`/`readCertPEM`, and demonstrate only synchronous CPU consumption from malformed ASN.1 length fields.
Because the qualifying finding does not reach the advisory's DSA nonce-generation logic or its incomplete bound checks, it is an open-world finding rather than an advisory match.
